narrative: Dear [Student's Name], I understand how disappointing it must be to receive a rejection from the law school, especially when you've worked hard to get here with your upper-middle family background, solid LSAT score of 37, and plans to attend full-time like most students. The admissions model predicted a 41% chance that you might fail the bar exam on your first try, which is high enough to tip the decision against your application, as schools prioritize applicants likely to pass and succeed. This prediction was driven mostly by your academic performance in school, particularly your Year 3 decile ranking of 2, which is well below the typical student's 5.5 and had the strongest influence in pushing the model toward expecting a bar failure. Right behind that was your Year 1 decile of 3, compared to the average of about 5.7, making it the second-biggest factor signaling potential struggles ahead. Your family income bracket came next in influence, as upper-middle status sometimes correlates with certain performance patterns in the data, nudging the prediction further toward failure. Your racial background as Asian, which is less common in the dataset where most students are White, played a moderate role in the same direction, followed by your LSAT score, undergraduate GPA of 2.8 (lower than the average 3.2), gender as male, and full-time status, each contributing progressively less but still tilting toward the riskier outcome. Overall, your lower high school deciles and undergrad GPA stood out as the top concerns when compared to typical successful students, while your LSAT was a relative bright spot that didn't quite offset them. These factors ranked in that order—Year 3 decile first, then Year 1, family income, race, LSAT, GPA, gender, and full-time—showing where the model saw the biggest red flags for bar success. The good news is that some of these, like building stronger study habits demonstrated by your deciles, point to areas you can improve. To boost your future chances, focus on targeted bar exam prep courses, practice tests, and time management skills, as these have helped many students overcome similar early academic hurdles. Retaking the LSAT if feasible could further strengthen your profile, since yours was already competitive. With dedication, you can shift the odds in your favor for the next application cycle—many have turned predictions like this around through focused effort. Keep pushing; your determination will shine through.
